title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Protecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiakitanga: Te Tiaki i Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is centered on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe and holds deep cultural, spiritual, and ecological importance for Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will explore the connection between the health of the awa and the wellbeing of our community, drawing on local pūrākau (stories) and mātauranga Māori related to water guardianship.
learningObjectives: Investigate and explain the connection between the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and the holistic wellbeing (hauora) of the local community, using a whare tapa whā model.
Collect and analyze water quality data from a designated site along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific methods and compare findings with historical and cultural indicators of stream health.

Collaboratively design and propose a practical kaitiakitanga action plan to address an identified issue affecting the awa, incorporating both mātauranga Māori and contemporary health and environmental perspectives.
Present findings and proposals to peers and a local kaumatua, demonstrating understanding of the role of kaitiakitanga in promoting community and environmental health.

activities.title: He Whakapapa o Te Awa | The Story of Our Stream
activities.description: Students engage with a local kaumatua or kaitiaki who shares the pūrākau and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. This is followed by a mapping activity where students identify key sites along the awa, historical land uses, and potential sources of impact, connecting these to the four dimensions of hauora (whare tapa whā).

activities.title: Te Tirohanga Matai | The Scientific and Cultural Investigation
activities.description: In small groups, students visit a pre-determined site on the awa. Using water testing kits, they collect data on pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and macroinvertebrate presence. Simultaneously, they make cultural observations using a provided framework (e.g., clarity of water, presence of native species, signs of life) and record their findings.

activities.title: Te Whakataurite | Data Analysis and Comparison
activities.description: Back in the classroom, groups analyze their scientific data, creating simple graphs. They compare this data with historical data sets (if available) and the cultural observations made on site. They discuss the discrepancies and correlations between the scientific and cultural measures of health, focusing on what this means for the wellbeing of the awa and the community.

activities.title: Te Mahere Kaitiaki | Designing the Action Plan
activities.description: Based on their analysis, each group identifies one key issue affecting the health of the awa. They then research and design a practical kaitiakitanga action plan to address it. The plan must include: a clear goal, actions rooted in both mātauranga Māori and science, required resources, and how the action will improve the hauora of the community and the environment.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga | Presentation and Feedback
activities.description: Groups present their findings and action plans to the class and a invited guest (e.g., kaumatua, representative from the local marae committee or Hawke's Bay Regional Council). They explain their reasoning and receive feedback, refining their plans based on the discussion.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council - Land Management Team (for water testing kits and potential guest speaker)
Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated website and resources, specifically those related to environmental management and te mana o te wai
Local Kaumatua or Kaitiaki from the Mangakōtukutuku area (for cultural guidance and storytelling)
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) digital resources and guides

tasks: A written or multimedia report analyzing the collected water quality data and cultural observations, explaining the connections to community hauora.
A group presentation of the proposed kaitiakitanga action plan, justifying the chosen approach with evidence from both scientific and cultural investigations.
A individual reflection on the process, detailing their personal understanding of kaitiakitanga and its importance to health and wellbeing.

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: Strand A - Personal Health and Physical Development (A3 - Safety management; A4 - Personal identity); Strand C - Relationships with Other People (C2 - Identity, sensitivity, and respect); Strand D - Healthy Communities and Environments (D1 - Societal attitudes and values; D3 - Community resources; D4 - Rights, responsibilities, and laws)
Science: Nature of Science - Participating and Contributing; Living World - Life Processes, Ecology
